ofcs.ofc_text: Consult your local PSA for information about the "If You See Something, Say Something™" public awareness campaign. For more information, visit the DHS Website at http://www.dhs.gov/if-you-see-something-say-something%E2%84%A2-campaign .

Access video and audio public service announcements encouraging the public to contact local authorities if they see suspicious activity at http://www.dhs.gov/if-you-see-something-say-something-public-service-announcements .

DHS is working to expand the "If You See Something, Say Something™" campaign throughout the country by partnering with a variety of entities including transportation systems, universities, States, cities, sports leagues, and local law enforcement. If you are interested in getting your group involved, please contact 202-282-8010.
Consult your local PSA for information about available "If You See Something, Say Something™" materials/messaging.
Consult your local PSA for information about PS-Prep™. Information is also available through the FEMA Website, at http://www.fema.gov/about-ps-preptm .
Research, review, and evaluate established risk management standards. Select the approach that is most relevant to the facility's particular circumstances.

Engage the risk manager/risk management department in the research, review, and evaluation of established risk management standards. Select the approach that is most relevant to the facility's particular circumstances.
Consult your local PSA for information about established risk management standards.

In May 2014, DHS announced the adoption of the following updated preparedness standards: ASIS International SPC.1-2009, Organizational Resilience: Security, Preparedness and Continuity Management Systems – Requirements with Guidance for Use Standards, ISO 22301:2012: Societal security -- Business continuity management systems, and NFPA 1600: Disaster/Emergency Management and Business Continuity Programs, 2013 edition . More information about these standards is available through the FEMA Website, at http://www.fema.gov/about-ps-preptm .

Establish an MOU/MOA with the agency to define the assistance and special services the agency will provide in the event of a threat, attack, or incident. An MOU/MOA can address items such as points of contact, notification procedures, exchange of threat information, access to the facility, hazards present on the facility, and participation in regular consults, drills, and exercises.
Establish a liaison with the agency. Explore the option of creating a formal agreement for assistance and special services.
Invite the agency to visit the facility. Provide a tour to familiarize first responders with the site layout.
Establish annual onsite visits for first responders to maintain their familiarity with the facility.
Collaborate with the agency on potential solutions to achieve cost-effective interoperable communications onsite.

Explore the option of an interconnect system, such as a gateway that can allow communication between radio systems that are otherwise incompatible because they operate on different frequency bands or using different technologies.

Install permanent storm shutters to protect windows to help prevent glass breakage and stop windborne objects from penetrating the facility. Always use permanent storm shutters in areas where there will be little advance warning of high winds, as permanent shutters can be closed quickly, unlike temporary plywood covers that must be retrieved from storage and mounted with bolts or screws. Consult the FEMA flyer Protect Windows and Doors with Covers , available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-8875/how2017_how2029_protect_windows_doors_4_11.pdf , for more information.

Explore options to retrofit roofing to secure it to the structure and increase resistance to high winds. Consult the FEMA Webpage "Protect Your Property from High Winds," at http://www.fema.gov/media-library/assets/documents/13270?id=3263 , for more information.
Securely anchor rooftop equipment (e.g., HVAC units, lightning protection systems, antennae) to prevent them from detaching during high winds.
Conduct a vulnerability assessment for electrical and mechanical utilities and systems to understand the facility's susceptibility to damage due to hurricane-related flooding.

Explore flood-proofing retrofits such as dry floodproofing (i.e., measures resulting in watertight, flood-resistant structures), wet flood-proofing (i.e., the use of flood-damage-resistant materials and construction techniques to minimize flood damage), floodwalls, and levees. Consult Floodproofing Non-Residential Buildings (FEMA P-936) , available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/9a50c534fc5895799321dcdd4b6083e7/P-936_8-20-13_508r.pdf , for more information.
Relocate utility installations and other critical assets away from rooms and locations that are below-grade and susceptible to flooding-related damage.

Protect the facility's backup power generator and its fuel tank, as well as electrical and fuel supply lines, from destructive natural hazards. In flood hazard areas, mount the generator and tank securely on concrete platforms, above the expected flood level. If possible, move the generator and tank inside or next to a building or protective structure to shield them from severe weather events such as wind. Consult the FEMA flyer Install a Generator for Emergency Power , available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-0643/how2022_generator_04_11_r1.pdf , for more information.

Explore the feasibility of procuring a backup power generator to power life safety systems in the event that a natural hazard disrupts power to the facility and its critical assets. Consult the FEMA flyer Install a Generator for Emergency Power , available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-0643/how2022_generator_04_11_r1.pdf , for more information.
Install the necessary wiring and transfer switches to connect to a backup power generator and plan for its rapid deployment.

Explore the feasibility of using natural gas to fuel the backup power system. While diesel, which is traditionally used to fuel backup power systems, is relatively safe to store onsite and economical to procure, re-supply for an extended period may be an issue. Because most natural gas infrastructure is underground, the natural gas supply may not be impacted by severe weather events. Source 1,
Consult the Ready.gov Website, at http://www.ready.gov/hurricanes , for more information.

Establish specific plans and procedures for the duration of hurricane season. Contact the State/local emergency management office and/or the regional FEMA office for available hurricane planning resources.

Develop shelter-in-place plans for facility occupants if it is not safe to evacuate the facility during a natural disaster. Train employees on the plan, and conduct recurring exercises on, reviews of, and updates to the plan. Ensure contractors and frequent visitors to the facility are aware of these plans. Consult the Ready.gov Website, available at http://www.ready.gov/shelter , for more information on sheltering.
Establish a hurricane planning checklist that can be provided to all facility occupants ahead of hurricane season.
Develop a plan for dealing with power outages and loss of elevator service in high-rise buildings. Ensure the plan includes the location of alternate exits for occupants.

Ensure all trees on facility property are far enough away to prevent them from damaging the facility if they should fall. Consult the FEMA flyer Remove Trees and Potential Windborne Missiles , available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-3107/how2020_trees_missiles_4_11.pdf , for more information.
Keep limbs on nearby trees trimmed to prevent breakage. Falling tree limbs can damage facilities, assets, and critical utility lines.

If the facility does not have a backup generator, explore options to enter into a contract to have one readily available, in the event that a natural hazard disrupts power to the facility and its critical assets. Ensure that the facility is equipped (i.e., has the necessary wiring and transfer switches) to facilitate the rapid installation and operation of the generator unit.

If the facility does not have permanent storm shutters, establish plans to board windows with exterior-grade or marine plywood. Ensure temporary covers are readily accessible, and use a numbering or lettering system that shows which cover goes with which window.

Establish plans to deploy emergency flood mitigation measures, such as sandbags, temporary flood barriers, and flood wrapping systems. Consult Floodproofing Non-Residential Buildings (FEMA P-936) , available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/9a50c534fc5895799321dcdd4b6083e7/P-936_8-20-13_508r.pdf , for more information.
Conduct a vulnerability assessment for electrical and mechanical utilities and systems to understand the facility's susceptibility to damage due to severe flooding.

Protect the facility's backup power generator and its fuel tank, as well as electrical and fuel supply lines, from destructive natural hazards. In flood hazard areas, mount the generator and tank securely on concrete platforms, above the expected flood level. Consult the FEMA flyer Install a Generator for Emergency Power , available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-0643/how2022_generator_04_11_r1.pdf , for more information.
Consult the Ready.gov Website, at http://www.ready.gov/floods , for more information.

Establish specific plans and procedures to protect the facility and its critical assets from flooding damage. Contact the State/local emergency management office and/or the regional FEMA office for available flood planning resources.
Establish a flood response checklist that can be provided to employees ahead of a flood watch and/or warning.
Consult the Ready.gov Website, at http://www.ready.gov/floods , for more information.

Explore the feasibility of modifying or retrofitting the facility to make it safer during earthquakes and more resistant to earthquake damage and disruption. The optimal combination earthquake risk-reduction measures for a facility will depend on factors such as the potential severity of the earthquake hazards in the area, the current condition of the facility, whether the facility is owned or leased, and how vulnerable facility operations are to facility damage and associated downtime. Consult QuakeSmart, at http://www.fema.gov/quakesmart, for information about how to assess facility risks and how to develop and implement a plan to cost-effectively mitigate those risks.

If the facility has cripple walls, brace the walls by adding 2-inch by 4-inch boards between vertical studs at the top and bottom of the walls and, if necessary, at other locations between the studs. Consult the FEMA flyer Brace Cripple Walls , available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-0271/how2012_brace_cripple_walls.pdf , for more information.

Anchor any sill plates. Sill plates rest directly on top of a foundation, and bolting the plates directly into the foundation provides extra stability. Consult the FEMA flyer Bolt Sill Plates to Foundation , available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-5339/how2013_sill_plates.pdf , for more information.

Explore the feasibility of retrofitting existing gas or water lines with flexible connections to prevent damage. Consult the FEMA flyer Use Flexible Connection on Gas and Water Lines , available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-7131/how2014_flex_connections.pdf , for more information.

Explore the feasibility of anchoring heavy equipment directly to the floor or another suitable part of the building. If this is not possible, mount equipment on vibration isolators. Consult the FEMA flyer Anchor Equipment Properly , available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-9548/how2023_anchor_equip.pdf , for more information.

Establish earthquake emergency plans and procedures. Emphasize safe places for building occupants to "drop, cover, and hold on" during earthquake shaking. Establish safe locations where people can rendezvous when the shaking has stopped and it is safe and advisable to evacuate the facility. Contact the State/local emergency management office and/or the regional FEMA office for available earthquake planning resources.
Conduct periodic, mandatory earthquake drills for employees to practice earthquake response procedures and condition themselves to react spontaneously and safely.
Arrange for employees to be trained in first aid, CPR, and fire extinguisher use to help protect facility occupants in the immediate aftermath of an earthquake.

Explore opportunities to engage in Great ShakeOut Earthquake Drills, which are annual events for communities to collectively practice and improve earthquake preparedness. Consult the Great ShakeOut Earthquake Drills Website, at http://www.shakeout.org, for more information.
Anchor overhead lighting fixtures, brace top-heavy objects, fasten all wall objects securely, and place all large or heavy objects in low positions (e.g., on the floor, lower shelves).
Consult the Ready.gov Website, at http://www.ready.gov/earthquakes, for more information.

Explore the feasibility of establishing a safe room or rooms specifically designed to meet FEMA criteria and provide "near-absolute protection" during tornadoes. To be considered a FEMA safe room, the structure must be designed and constructed to the guidelines specified in FEMA P-320, Taking Shelter from the Storm: Building a Safe Room for Your Home or Small Business (available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1454-20490-8914/fema_p_320.pdf ), and FEMA P-361, Design and Construction Guidance for Community Safe Rooms (available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1508-20490-8283/fema_p_361.pdf ). Consult the FEMA Webpage "Safe Rooms," at http://www.fema.gov/safe-rooms , for more information and additional resources.

Protect the facility's backup power generator and its fuel tank from high winds. Move the generator and tank inside or next to a building or protective structure to shield them from wind and windborne debris. Consult the FEMA flyer Install a Generator for Emergency Power , available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-0643/how2022_generator_04_11_r1.pdf , for more information.
Consult the Ready.gov Website, at http://www.ready.gov/tornadoes , for more information.
Establish tornado emergency plans and procedures. Contact the State/local emergency management office and/or the regional FEMA office for available tornado planning resources.
Conduct periodic, mandatory tornado drills for employees to practice tornado response procedures and condition themselves to react spontaneously and safely.
Consult the Ready.gov Website, at http://www.ready.gov/tornadoes, for more information.
Explore the feasibility of retrofitting the facility with fire-resistant materials such as tile, stucco, metal siding, brick, concrete block, and steel.
Treat wood or combustible materials used in roofs, siding, decking, or trim with fire-retardant chemicals evaluated by a nationally recognized laboratory.
Explore the feasibility of replacing skylights with fire-resistant-rated skylights and framing. Skylights are typically constructed with materials that can melt under intense heat.
Replace windows and glass doors with tempered safety glass.
Explore the feasibility of installing fire-resistant door assemblies to protect the facility from fires.
Protect vent openings that may allow burning embers to enter the facility.
Explore the feasibility of constructing a noncombustible retaining wall to help increase setback.
Design and landscape facility grounds home with wildfire safety in mind. Select materials and plants that are fire-resistant.
Relocate propane tanks at least 30 feet from the facility, and create a 10-foot noncombustible zone around them.
Consult the Ready.gov Website, at http://www.ready.gov/wildfires . For more information.

Establish specific plans and procedures to protect the facility and its critical assets from wildfires. Contact the State/local emergency management office and/or the regional FEMA office for available wildfire planning resources.

Conduct periodic, mandatory wildfire drills for employees to practice wildfire response procedures and condition themselves to react spontaneously and safely. Consult Prepare Your People: Wildfire Safety for Workplaces , available here , for more information.
Establish a liaison with the local fire department or forestry office to remain alert of hazardous conditions.
Explore the feasibility of adding shallow rooftop pools for firefighting during wildfire season.
Explore the feasibility of installing sprinklers, water storage tanks, and automated pumps with dedicated generators to coincide with wildfire season.
Establish a maintenance plan for irrigation systems, hoses, and generators.
Establish a maintenance plan to clear all flammable vegetation (e.g., dead branches, leaves, twigs) from facility grounds during wildfire season.
Keep a 30- to 100-foot safety zone around the facility during wildfire season where facility occupants could go to survive a wildfire in the vicinity.
Remove all wooden objects (e.g., shingles, non-essential equipment components) from the exterior of the facility.
Consult the Ready.gov Website, at http://www.ready.gov/wildfires , for more information.
Establish the following plans and procedures in anticipation of an imminent wildfire:
If advised to evacuate, do so immediately.
Wet shrubs within 15 feet of the facility.
Shut down all utilities, if possible.
Shut off any natural gas, propane, or fuel oil supplies at the source.
Explore the feasibility of retrofitting the facility to withstand snow loads and prevent roof collapse.

Winterize the facility by insulating walls, caulking, and weather-stripping doors and windows, and installing storm windows or covering windows with plastic. Such measures can extend the life of the fuel supply.
Retrofit water pipes with enhanced insulation to prevent freezing.
For more information, visit the Ready.gov Website, at http://www.ready.gov/winter-weather .

Establish specific plans and procedures for severe winter storms. Contact the State/local emergency management office and/or the regional FEMA office for available severe winter storm planning resources.
Use snow fences or “living snow fences” (i.e., rows of trees or other vegetation) to limit blowing and drifting snow.
Develop plans and procedures to sand areas to improve traction, spread rock salt to melt ice, and make snow removal equipment readily available.
Explore the feasibility of installing roadway heating technology to prevent ice/snow buildup.
Establish plans and procedures to shut off water pipes to prevent freezing and possibly bursting.
Insulate pipes with insulation or newspapers and plastic and allow faucets to drip slightly to avoid freezing.

Watch for warning signs of overstress conditions (e.g., sagging ceiling tiles, cracks in walls or masonry, and severe roof leaks) during events involving heavy snow. If conditions exist, evacuate personnel and request a structural inspection. Consult the FEMA flyer Snow Load Safety Guidance , available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/1392984631969-ac57339deb6ee839a52b16b01eeee53e/FEMA_Snow_Load_508.pdf , for more information.

Remove snow from the roof, if possible, following proper OSHA protocol for working on rooftops to ensure safety. Heavy snow accumulation can damage a facility's roof and possibly cause it to collapse.
Inspect drains, gutters, downspouts, and vents for snow or ice blockage from earlier snowfalls to make room for new snow.
Install lightning protection devices and methods, such as lightning rods and grounding stakes/cables, on communications infrastructure and other critical facilities.

Ensure that any installed lightning protection systems are attached properly and can withstand high winds that may accompany thunder storms. Consult the FEMA flyer Rooftop Attachment of Lightning Protection Systems in High-Wind Regions , available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1605-20490-6583/fema549_apndx_e_ra7.pdf , for more information.
Install and maintain surge protection on critical electronic equipment.
Consult the Ready.gov Website, at http://www.ready.gov/thunderstorms-lightning , for more information.
Post signage around the facility to promote awareness of lightning dangers.
Train employees on the dangers of lightning and what safety precautions to take if caught in a lightning storm.

Consult your local PSA regarding opportunities to enhance information sharing with the FBI. The FBI has a variety of major information sharing initiatives for sharing timely, relevant, and actionable intelligence. For more information, visit the FBI Website at http://www.fbi.gov/stats-services/publications/national-information-sharing-strategy-1 .

Consult your local police department, sheriff's office, and/or PSA regarding opportunities to enhance information sharing with the JTTF. Information about JTTFs is also available via the FBI Website, at http://www.fbi.gov/about-us/investigate/terrorism/terrorism_jttfs .
Establish a liaison with the FBI to enhance communications, coordination, and cooperation.
Consult your local PSA regarding opportunities to enhance information sharing with the appropriate Federal law enforcement agencies.
Consult your local police department, sheriff's office, and/or PSA regarding opportunities to enhance information sharing with the appropriate Federal law enforcement agencies.

Consult your local PSA regarding opportunities to enhance information sharing with the JTTF. Information about JTTFs is also available via the FBI Website, at http://www.fbi.gov/about-us/investigate/terrorism/terrorism_jttfs .

Consult your local police department, sheriff's office, and/or PSA regarding opportunities to enhance information sharing with the JTTF. Information about JTTFs is also available via the FBI Website, at http://www.fbi.gov/about-us/investigate/terrorism/terrorism_jttfs .
Consult your local PSA regarding opportunities to enhance information sharing with the ATF.
Consult your local police department, sheriff's office, and/or PSA regarding opportunities to enhance information sharing with the ATF.
Establish a liaison with the ATF to enhance communications, coordination, and cooperation.

Consult your local PSA regarding opportunities to enhance information sharing with the appropriate ISAC. Additional information is available via the National Council of ISACs Website, at http://www.isaccouncil.org .
Consult your local PSA to become a member of the HSIN community. Information about HSIN is also available via the DHS Website, at http://www.dhs.gov/homeland-security-information-network.
Consult your local PSA to better understand how HSIN's features and capabilities can enhance information sharing with your mission partners.
Consult your local PSA about becoming a member of InfraGard. Additional information is also available via the InfraGard Website, at https://www.infragard.org .
Consult your local PSA regarding opportunities to enhance information sharing with the U.S. Attorney's Office ATAC.
Consult your local police department, sheriff's office, and/or PSA regarding opportunities to enhance information sharing with the U.S. Attorney's Office ATAC.
Contact your district U.S. Attorney's Office regarding opportunities to enhance information sharing with the ATAC.
Consult your local PSA regarding opportunities to enhance information sharing with DHS.

Consult your local PSA regarding opportunities to enhance information sharing with NOAA. Guidance on the use of and access to National Weather Service watches, warnings, advisories, and other similar products is available via the NOAA Website, at http://alerts.weather.gov .

Consult your local PSA regarding opportunities to enhance information sharing with USGS. Information about USGS data catalogs and portals is available via the USGS Website, at http://www.usgs.gov/datamanagement/share/usgscatalogs.php .
Consult your local PSA regarding opportunities to enhance information sharing with the CDC.

The CDC’s PHIN is a national initiative to increase the capacity of public health agencies to electronically exchange data and information across organizations and jurisdictions (e.g., clinical care to public health, public health to public health and public health to other federal agencies). Contact PHIN by phone or email, at 770-454-4863 or PHINTECH@CDC.GOV, or visit the CDC Website for more information, at http://www.cdc.gov/phin/about/index.html .

The CDC Office for State, Tribal, Local and Territorial Support includes the CoPs Program: CoPs provide a collaborative framework for public health professionals to share information and expertise. To join a CoP, sign up at http://www.phconnect.org or visit the Join a CoP Webpage, at http://www.cdc.gov/phcommunities/join.html .

Consult your local PSA regarding opportunities to enhance information sharing with Federal agencies, such as the FBI and other Federal law enforcement (e.g., FPS, TSA, ICE), JTTF, ATF, InfraGard, U.S. Attorney's Office ATAC, DHS, NOAA, USGS, and CDC.

Consult your local police department, sheriff's office, and/or PSA regarding opportunities to enhance information sharing with Federal agencies, such as the FBI and other Federal law enforcement (e.g., FPS, TSA, ICE), JTTF, ATF, InfraGard, U.S. Attorney's Office ATAC, DHS, NOAA, USGS, and CDC.
Consult your local PSA regarding opportunities to enhance information sharing with State and/or major urban area fusion centers.
Consult your local police department, sheriff's office, and/or PSA regarding opportunities to enhance information sharing with State and/or major urban area fusion centers.
Establish a liaison with State and/or major urban area fusion centers to enhance communications, coordination, and cooperation.
Consult your local PSA regarding opportunities to enhance information sharing with the State CIP Coordinator.
Consult your local PSA regarding opportunities to enhance information sharing with the State HSA.
Consult your local PSA regarding opportunities to enhance information sharing with the State EMA.
Consult your local police department, sheriff's office, and/or PSA regarding opportunities to enhance information sharing with the State EMA.
Consult your local PSA regarding opportunities to enhance information sharing with State law enforcement.
Consult your local police department, sheriff's office, and/or PSA regarding opportunities to enhance information sharing with State law enforcement.
Establish a liaison with State law enforcement to enhance information exchange, clarify emergency response roles, track threat conditions, and support investigations.

Coordinate the facility’s security and emergency response plans with State law enforcement. Share critical information about the facility (e.g., floor plans, location of critical assets or areas, and notification and contact lists).
Consult your local PSA regarding opportunities to enhance information sharing with the local EMA.
Consult your local police department, sheriff's office, and/or PSA regarding opportunities to enhance information sharing with the local EMA.
Consult your local PSA regarding opportunities to enhance information sharing with local law enforcement.
Establish a liaison with local law enforcement to enhance information exchange, clarify emergency response roles, track threat conditions, and support investigations.

Coordinate the facility’s security and emergency response plans with local law enforcement. Share critical information about the facility (e.g., floor plans, location of critical assets or areas, and notification and contact lists).
Consult your local PSA regarding opportunities to enhance information sharing with industry groups.
Consult your local PSA regarding opportunities to enhance information sharing with public health agencies.
Consult with management personnel regarding opportunities to enhance information sharing with corporate law enforcement or security.

Consult your local PSA regarding opportunities to enhance information sharing with State/local agencies, such as State and/or major urban area fusion centers, State CIP Coordinator, State HSA, State/local EMA, State/local law enforcement, and State/local public health agencies.

Consult your local police department, sheriff's office, and/or PSA regarding opportunities to enhance information sharing with State/local agencies, such as State and/or major urban area fusion centers, State CIP Coordinator, State HSA, State/local EMA, State/local law enforcement, and State/local public health agencies.

Assess the need to establish prearrangements to augment facility resources during elevated threat situations, emergencies, and other circumstances. Collaborate with the appropriate agencies to establish written agreements for assistance.

Consult your local PSA for more information about the DHS Private Sector Clearance Program, which sponsors security clearances for certain private-sector officials, including critical infrastructure owners/operators, sector leadership, and subject matter experts.
Incorporate a threat level system into the security plan.

Incorporate the DHS NTAS into the security plan. The NTAS provides information timely, detailed information about terrorist threats to the public, government agencies, first responders, airports and other transportation hubs, and the private sector. Consult the DHS NTAS Website, at http://www.dhs.gov/national-terrorism-advisory-system , for more information.

Incorporate MARSEC levels into the security plan. MARSEC is a three-tiered system employed by the USGS to communicate with maritime industry partners of potential pre-planned responses to threats. MARSEC levels adjust in conjunction with NTAS alerts. Consult the USGS MARSEC Levels Website, at http://www.uscg.mil/safetylevels/whatismarsec.asp , for more information.
Incorporate an industry threat level system into the security plan.

Explore the feasibility of employing and implementing additional protective measures to increase the overall security posture of the facility as threat levels increase. Options may include additional access controls; barriers; increased communications and notification; enhanced cyber security; additional infrastructure upgrades and redundancy; enhanced incident response; additional monitoring and surveillance detection; and planning and preparedness.

Conduct planning and coordination and gather resources to implement additional protective measures appropriate for the facility during elevated threat situations in a timely manner. Train facility personnel on their responsibilities associated with these protective measures, and exercise these measures annually to evaluate facility preparedness and program effectiveness.
Employ the following additional protective measures during elevated threat situations:
Establish additional access controls
Restrict access to essential personnel only
Conduct inspections/searches
Decrease the number of personnel authorized to be onsite
Prevent onsite access by visitors
Prevent parking onsite
Minimize the number of gates in use
Require visitor escorts
Employ or enforce parking restrictions
Prevent onsite access by visitors
Add additional barriers
Add barriers at facility access points
Add barriers at significant assets
Employ additional communications and notifications
Lock down control or operation centers
Establish real-time communication between security and decision-level executives
Coordinate security efforts with local responders
Coordinate security efforts with State responders
Coordinate security efforts with Federal responders
Lock-down control or operation centers
Enhanced cyber security
Install additional infrastructure upgrades and redundancies
Enhanced incident response (e.g., initiated MOU with Local Law Enforcement or Fire Department)
Employ additional monitoring and surveillance detection activities
Establish perimeter patrols
Increase the frequency of perimeter patrols
Employ continuous perimeter patrols
Enhance security force staffing
Increase security force staffing
Maximize security force staffing
Increase vehicle inspections
Increase vehicle inspections to 100%
Establish random vehicle inspections
Increase personnel inspections
Increase personnel inspections to 100%
Establish random personnel inspections
Hire or contract for additional security personnel
Provide additional illumination for remote areas
Add counter surveillance teams
Distribute night vision devices to security personnel
Increased vehicle inspections
Increased personnel inspections
Initiate planning and preparedness procedures
Pre-assign personnel (on-call)
Assign emergency response personnel to pre-planned positions/roles
Prepare to execute contingency procedures
Execute contingency procedures

Designate an employee to act as a security manager and task that person with developing, implementing, and coordinating all security-related activities. If possible, choose an employee with previous security experience. Consult The Critical Success Factor Method: Establishing a Foundation for Enterprise Security Management , available at http://www.ready.gov/document/critical-success-factor-method-establishing-foundation-enterprise-security-management-0 , for more information.

Develop a comprehensive security plan specific to the facility. Train personnel on the plan, and exercise the plan at least once a year. Consult The Critical Success Factor Method: Establishing a Foundation for Enterprise Security Management , available at http://www.ready.gov/document/critical-success-factor-method-establishing-foundation-enterprise-security-management-0 , for more information.

Develop a comprehensive security plan specific to the facility. The plan should address issues such as the following: protection of employees, contractors/vendors, visitors, and executives; protection of sensitive information; protection of funds; facility access control procedures; suspicious activity reporting procedures; employee termination procedures; parking; mail security; background checks; prohibited items; security force; end-of-day security checks; control and accountability of equipment (including keys); electronic security systems (including CCTV); physical security inspection programs; and employee security awareness training programs. Train personnel on the plan, and exercise the plan at least once a year.

Develop a comprehensive security plan specific to the facility. The plan should address topics such as the following: physical security measures and systems, security force, access control procedures, information protection, and security awareness training. In addition, the plan should include elements such as the following: an assessment of possible security risks; a review of threats to the facility and the facility's vulnerabilities; an up-to-date point of contact roster for key personnel responsible for security and first responders; and identification of critical assets or areas. Train personnel on the plan, and exercise the plan at least once a year.

Consult The Critical Success Factor Method: Establishing a Foundation for Enterprise Security Management , available at http://www.ready.gov/document/critical-success-factor-method-establishing-foundation-enterprise-security-management-0 , for more information.
Develop a facility-specific addendum to the corporate security plan. Source 1,
Develop a comprehensive security plan specific to the facility.

Acquire the signature and approval of a senior manager or designated representative for the security plan. Consult The Critical Success Factor Method: Establishing a Foundation for Enterprise Security Management , available at http://www.ready.gov/document/critical-success-factor-method-establishing-foundation-enterprise-security-management-0 , for more information. Source 1,

Develop a comprehensive business continuity plan to be implemented in the aftermath of an incident. The business continuity plan should contain the following elements: a) defined roles and responsibilities for personnel with authority during and following an incident; b) a process for activating the response; c) details to manage the immediate consequences of a disruptive incident (with regard to the welfare of individuals; strategic, tactical, and operational response options, and prevention of further loss or unavailability of prioritized activities); d) details on how and under what circumstances the facility will communicate with employees and their relatives, key interested parties, and emergency contacts; e) how the facility will continue or recover its prioritized activities within predetermined timeframes; f) details of the facility's media response following an incident; and g) a process for standing down once the incident is over. Train personnel on the plan, and exercise the plan at least once a year. For more information, visit the Ready.gov Website at http://www.ready.gov/business/implementation/continuity .

Develop a comprehensive business continuity plan to be implemented in the aftermath of an incident. The business continuity plan should identify stakeholders that need to be notified; critical and time-sensitive applications; alternative work sites; vital records, contact lists, processes, and functions that must be maintained; and personnel, procedures, and resources that are necessary while the facility recovers. Train personnel on the plan, and exercise the plan at least once a year. For more information, visit the Ready.gov Website at http://www.ready.gov/business/implementation/continuity .

Business continuity management must always start at the top – for the essential reason that business continuity management is about what’s important and time-sensitive. Source 1,

Explore the feasibility of establishing or contracting a security force to actively protect assets, property, and employees, and to rapidly respond to security incidents and/or emergencies. Provide the security force with comprehensive post orders and a dedicated command and control/operation center.

Detail in the surge capacity plan the roles, responsibilities, and chain of command for both regular and surge forces.

Vary routes and times of roving patrols at frequent intervals to preclude establishing a routine that may be observed by potential intruders.

Develop post orders that describe in detail the duties and responsibilities of the security force. After comprehensive post orders are developed, ensure that they are kept current and accessible. Comprehensive post orders may serve to express the policies of the protected enterprise, summarize required officer duties, avoid problems associated with of word-of-mouth instructions, and provide a basis for site-specific training. They are the vital link between the requirements of the facility and the ability of security personnel to effectively meet those requirements.

Increase the height of the fence or install outriggers with barbed wire to enhance the height and penetration delay provided by the fence, if more than boundary demarcation is appropriate. Consult Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06) , available at http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf , for more information.
Replace the existing fence with higher fencing appropriate for the facility type.

Increase the height of the fence or install outriggers with barbed wire to enhance the height and penetration delay provided by the fence, if more than boundary demarcation is appropriate. The fence should be at least 7-9 feet high. The higher the fence, the more difficult and time-consuming it is to climb.
Anchor the base of the fence to protect against access by crawling under or raising lower portion of fence. The fence should also be installed to preclude access made possible by ground erosion.

Extend the fencing fabric to within 2 inches of firm ground, and anchor the base of the fence using horizontal bottom rails, tension wires, concrete curbs, sills, sheet piling, piping, or other inexpensive materials. Horizontal bottom rails, concrete curbs, or sills can mitigate the ability to lift the fence fabric by hand (e.g., no more than 5 inches).

Bury the fence fabric (e.g., 12 inches into the ground) for additional security. This may also prevent the fencing fabric from being lifted (for example, by more than 5 inches). However, corrosion of the buried fabric must be monitored.

Equip the fence with outriggers that are angled out and away from the facility to make climbing the fence more difficult and to prevent ladders from being placed against it. The overhang should extend far enough to increase the height of the fence by at least 1 foot. Consult UFC 4-022-03, Security Fences and Gates , available at http://www.wbdg.org/ccb/DOD/UFC/ufc_4_022_03.pdf , and Access Control Point Standard Drawings and Criteria , available at https://pdc.usace.army.mil/library/drawings/acp , for more information. Source 1,

Install outriggers with connecting wire, such as three strands of barbed wire at regular intervals, along the top of the fence. Outriggers typically face outward (i.e., away from the facility) and upward at approximately a 45-degree angle.

Install outriggers (support arms) at 45-degree angles in a single arm (towards the threat side) or “Y”/”V” configuration, constructed of a single or double outrigger consisting of 18-inch (457 mm) arm(s), each having three strands of barbed wire at regular intervals along the top of the fence. The outriggers should provide a minimum of an additional 12 inches to the fence height. Consult Access Control Point Standard Drawings and Criteria , available at https://pdc.usace.army.mil/library/drawings/acp , for more information.
Install connecting wire, such as three strands of barbed wire, at regular intervals along the top of the fence.

Add slats or mesh fabric to the fence to provide screening, if privacy is appropriate. Such screening can provide opportunities for cover and concealment and preclude visual assessment by security force. However, these techniques should not be used if air flow through the fence is important.

Eliminate the top rail of a fence to make it more difficult to grasp. Source 1,
Integrate other security systems with the fence, including sensors, cameras, or other intrusion detection devices, as appropriate.
Explore the feasibility of integrating chain-link fabric into perimeter fencing which covers the chain-link fencing to prevent penetration and provide concealment.
Apply welded wire-mesh fabric to the fence. Welded wire-mesh fabric is welded tightly together to prevent toe or finger hold and adds greater resistance against cutting.
Explore the feasibility of adding barbed, taped concertina to the top of the fence. Taped concertina can significantly decrease the ability to scale a fence.

Explore the feasibility of installing "tangle-foot wire." Tangle-foot wire is barbed wire strung across two posts no higher than 12 inches. It is used outside a perimeter fence or between double fences to provide extra protection from an intruder. Liability issues should be considered when installing tangle-foot wire.
Explore the feasibility of adding tunneling preventive measures for gaps that would allow for entry under the fence.

Establish clear zones on both sides of the fence to provide an unobstructed view to enhance detection and assessment. The dimensions of clears zones may vary depending on the asset being protected and level of protection required. Provide as much open space as possible between the fence and the facility.

Ensure clear zones are free of debris, vegetation, and any items that could provide cover and concealment. When it is impossible to have adequate clear zones because of property lines or natural or manmade features, it may be necessary to increase the height of the perimeter barrier, increase security-patrol coverage, and/or add more security lighting along that portion of the perimeter. Failure to establish and maintain a clear zone along the fence minimizes its effectiveness. Consult Site and Urban Design for Security: Guidance against Potential Terrorist Attacks (FEMA 430), available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, for more information. Source 1 , Source 2,

Ensure that for high security applications a clear zone of 20 feet or more separates a perimeter barrier from exterior structures, parking areas, and natural features. A clear zone of 50 feet or more should separate a perimeter barrier from structures within the protected area, except when a building wall constitutes part of a perimeter barrier.Consult Site and Urban Design for Security: Guidance against Potential Terrorist Attacks (FEMA 430), available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, for more information.

Implement a grounds maintenance program to check and clear vegetation regularly from the fence's clear zone. Vegetation should not exceed 8 inches in height. Also, because fallen trees can damage fences, management of tall trees in the vicinity of the fence is important, especially in areas where a fence passes through a remote area.
Remove all objects near the fence that would aid traversing it. Keep a clear zone free of any item that could aid an intruder in breeching or circumventing the perimeter fence.
Ensure security force roving patrols include surveillance of the perimeter fence and objects/structures that remain near the fence line.

Install warning signs that are easy to understand along the fence. In areas where two or more languages are commonly spoken, the warning signs must use both (or more) languages. Post signage at intervals of no more than 100 feet.

Post visible, well-placed warning signs on the fence. Signs may act as a deterrent and/or provide safety information for unauthorized personnel. Signs are usually placed on boundary fences, typically at 50-foot intervals, to indicate ownership and to warn of possible danger within. In areas where two or more languages are commonly spoken, the warning signs must use both (or more) languages.

Install vehicle gates for restricted areas so that they are set back from the roadway to ensure that temporary delays caused by identification checks will not cause traffic hazards. Integrate other security systems, including sensors, cameras, or other intrusion detection devices, at gates, as appropriate.
Replace drop-arm gates with more robust gates that provide appropriate levels of access control and/or penetration delay.

Install access control systems and/or implement access control procedures designed to permit only authorized access, detect and prevent contraband from being brought into the facility, and provide information to security personnel to facilitate the appropriate assessment and response. Effective access control can be established through the integrated use of guards, locks, credentials, and/or screening equipment (e.g., x-ray scanners, magnetometers).
Establish the following access controls:

Post security guards at entrances to facilitate access control procedures such as credential checks and searches. If this is not possible, integrate CCTV or other surveillance equipment to facilitate remote monitoring of access points.

Implement screening operations for the facility. Available screening options include, but are not limited to, x-ray scanners, magnetometers, package searches, and physical searches. Post signage to notify potential entrants that screening is a prerequisite for entry into the facility.
Issue photo ID badges to all employees. Implement a process for badge verification before employees gain access to the facility. Require badges to be displayed onsite at all times.
Review all requests for visitor access.
Evaluate the need to screen visitor requests (e.g., with local law enforcement) to identify issues before authorizing access.
Maintain a list of regular visitors to the facility.
Limit visitor access to the facility at a level consistent with onsite safety and security requirements.
Require visitors to sign in and sign out.
Issue badges to visitors and require badges to be displayed at all times at the facility. Collect badges when visits are complete.
Escort visitors as necessary, either at all times or on+E36ly in sensitive/critical areas.
Maintain a list of regular contractors/vendors.
Limit contractor/vendor access to the facility at a level consistent with onsite safety and security requirements.
Require contractors/vendors to sign in and sign out.
Issue badges to contractors/vendors and require badges to be displayed at all times at the facility. Collect badges when visits are complete.
Escort contractors/vendors as necessary, either at all times or only in sensitive/critical areas.
Limit customer/patron/public access to the facility at a level consistent with onsite safety and security requirements.
Post security guards at entrances to facilitate access control procedures such as credential checks and searches on special event days.

Implement screening operations for the facility. Available screening options include, but are not limited to, x-ray scanners, magnetometers, bag searches, and physical searches. Post signage to notify potential entrants that screening is a prerequisite for entry into the facility.

Issue photo ID badges to all employees. Implement a process for badge verification before employees gain access to the facility. Collect employee badges from terminated personnel.
Issue unique badges (e.g., color-coded) to visitors, contractors, vendors, cleaning crews, and temporary employees. Collect badges when visits are complete.
Require badges to be displayed at all times at the facility.
Enforce badging system requirements consistently.

Explore options to establish access levels (i.e., to sensitive or critical areas) using the keycard system.
Evaluate the need for dedicated, trained security staff to continuously monitor keycard access control system alarm and event activity.

Explore options to restrict access to sensitive/critical areas using a keycard system.

Identify areas within the facility that need additional access controls. Implement controls (e.g., locks) for these areas as necessary.
Explore options to establish access levels to sensitive/critical areas using the existing keycard system.
Explore options to establish access levels to sensitive/critical areas using a keycard system.
Establish a key control program that includes a system for the retrieval of keys from terminated personnel and a formal inventory of key assignments.

Limit the use of master keys for secured sensitive or critical areas to select security personnel.
Designate a key control officer to manage the key control program and conduct regular key inventories.

Post security guards at vehicle entrances to facilitate access control procedures such as credential checks and searches. If this is not possible, integrate CCTV or other surveillance equipment to facilitate remote monitoring of vehicle access points.

Explore the feasibility of executing searches on all vehicles entering the controlled parking area for the facility.
Execute random searches on vehicles entering the controlled parking area for the facility. Evaluate the need to conduct searches on all vehicles at elevated threat levels.

Positively identify all vehicles and drivers that enter the facility. Deny access to suspicious vehicles/drivers and those with improper documentation (e.g., cargo manifest). Deny access to drivers who fail to provide identification and/or submit to inspection.
Maintain a database of employee vehicles. Issue permits for designated areas.
Evaluate the feasibility of using centralized parking and shuttle services to keep vehicles away from critical areas.

Explore options to restrict parking within 400 feet of the facility, to minimize consequences from potential VBIEDs.

Allow only permit parking by authorized individuals of inspected vehicles within 400 feet of the facility.

(1) Determine the facility’s susceptibility to blast. Consult Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06) , available at http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf , for more information, especially Chapters 2 and 3: Chapter 2 discusses blast design concerns and protective measures for areas surrounding buildings. Chapter 3 discusses the nature of explosive blasts, their effects on buildings and occupants, and the concept of levels of protection. In addition, review the DHS Bomb Threat Stand-off Chart , available through the Lessons Learned Information Sharing national, online network, at https://www.llis.dhs.gov/sites/default/files/DHS-BombThreatChart-6-5-09.pdf . (2) Based on the facility’s potential vulnerabilities, explore the feasibility of conducting a preliminary blast analysis using simple, commercially available building modeling and software designed to support the ability to address blast mitigation and potential VBIED threats, such as A.T.-BLAST by ARA Applied Research Associates. (3) Based on the results from the preliminary blast analysis, explore the feasibility of consulting with an engineering firm that specializes in structural fragility to procure a professional blast assessment to inform capital investments intended to protect personnel and the facility against blast.
Explore the feasibility of installing high curbs and other measures to keep vehicles from departing the roadway in an effort to avoid security countermeasures.
Explore the feasibility of restricting or eliminating parking along the curb, and/or eliminating street-side loading zones for high-risk assets.
Request appropriate permits to restrict parking in curb lanes in densely populated areas to company-owned vehicles or key employee vehicles.

Explore the feasibility of prohibiting street parking or enacting lane closure as a temporary measure during times of increased alert. Carefully plan such closures against elevated threats to avoid improvising with unaesthetic and disruptive measures.
Parking within the building leaves the facility highly susceptible to accidental or intentional damage, but if parking elsewhere is not an option, apply the following restrictions:
Permit parking only for company vehicles and employees of the building.
Require proper credentials for all passengers.
Conduct full vehicle inspections.
Require ID checks for visitor parking.
Ensure under-building parking is well-lighted and free of places of concealment and dead-end parking spaces.
Maximize visibility for surveillance into, out of, and across the parking garage.

Explore the feasibility of providing 24/7 CCTV monitoring for the parking area. Although CCTV coverage may need to be limited in its scope, it can still provide satisfactory coverage. Source 1, Source 2,

Install cameras in areas such as the main entrance, at all vehicle access control points, guard booths, cashier booths, and elevator lobbies on every floor. This will ensure more than adequate security. Source 1, Source 2,
Position the cameras to view the parking ramps and driveways as well as the intercom stations.
Ensure lighting adequately illuminates activity in the parking area for viewing and videotaping.

Ensure that the parking area is covered by roving patrols conducted by the security force. Routes and times for patrols should also be varied at frequent intervals to preclude establishing a routine that may be observed by potential intruders. Source 1,
Explore the feasibility of using facility employees to monitor uncontrolled parking areas.

Provide suspicious activity awareness training to facility employees, including topics related to uncontrolled parking areas. Provide options (e.g., hotline, direct phone or radio communications with security operations) for employees to report suspicious activity, potential threats, and incidents.

Implement SOPs to address unauthorized, suspicious, and extended-stay vehicles. Processes to consider include reporting vehicles to security and/or local law enforcement, and contracting with an area towing company for vehicle removal.
Train security personnel on the SOPs to address unauthorized, suspicious, and extended-stay vehicles.
Ensure that the parking area is covered by roving patrols conducted by the security force to ensure early identification of unauthorized parked vehicles. Source 1
Explore options to staff the controlled parking area. If possible, employ security personnel to screen drivers and/or inspect vehicles.

If SOPs for vehicle inspections are implemented, ensure that inspection areas are large enough to accommodate a minimum of one vehicle and a pull-out lane. They should also be covered and capable of accommodating the inspection of the undercarriage plus overhead inspection equipment. Inspection bays that can be closed to protect inspection equipment and staff in the event of bad weather are ideal.
Investigate available inspection technologies (e.g., above-vehicle and under-vehicle surveillance systems, ion scanning, and x-ray equipment) when sizing and designing vehicle inspection areas.
Evaluate the need to provide security staffing for vehicle access control points. Security force personnel may be trained and authorized to conduct vehicle searches and deny vehicle access.
Explore the feasibility of executing searches on all vehicles entering the controlled parking area for the facility.
Execute random searches on vehicles entering the controlled parking area for the facility. Evaluate the need to conduct searches on all vehicles at elevated threat levels.
Employ a locking mechanism to help ensure that the controlled parking area remains secured during business hours.
Employ a locking mechanism to help ensure that the controlled parking area remains secured during off-business hours.
Evaluate vehicle traffic patterns near the facility. Design and implement strategies to reduce vehicle speeds, improve pedestrian safety, and reduce the threat of vehicle approach velocities.

Install barriers to mitigate high-speed avenues of approach, deny vehicle entry, and provide perimeter protection. Options include, but are not limited to, fixed and retractable bollards, engineered planters, heavy objects and trees, walls and ha-ha barriers, water obstacles, and Jersey barriers.
Install fixed bollards, engineered planters, and/or heavy objects to mitigate high-speed avenues of approach.

Install a bollard system that is well-integrated into the perimeter security design and the streetscape to minimize visual impact. Bollard height should typically not be more than 30 inches. Bollard spacing should be between 36 and 43 inches, depending on the kind of traffic expected and the needs of pedestrians and the handicapped.

Install planters with considerable reinforcing and below-grade depth for effectiveness. Maintain a maximum distance of 4 feet between planters and other permanent streetscape elements, depending on the kind of traffic anticipated. Orient planters in a direction parallel to the curb or primary flow of pedestrian traffic. Do not place planter or a line of planters perpendicular to the curb.

Install heavy objects that can resist vehicle penetration, such as boulders, sculptures, and trees. Such objects need varying degrees of embedment and reinforcement, depending on their weight, footprint, and height/width ratio.

Explore the possibility of erecting walls, which includes retaining walls and freestanding walls, that may be constructed of concrete, concrete masonry, brick, natural stone, or other materials typically reinforced with steel.
Install Jersey barriers. To be effective, Jersey barriers need to be embedded and include vertical anchorage of steel reinforcing through the barrier into the pavement.
Install Jersey barriers as a temporary solution. Jersey barriers are relatively inexpensive and readily available.
Explore the possibility of adding speed controls (e.g., serpentines or speed bumps) to limit vehicle speed at impact.

Explore the possibility of installing active barriers, such as retractable bollards or a wedge system, to mitigate high-speed avenues of approach. Active barriers must be tended at all times by trained personnel and require regular maintenance to ensure continued operation.

Explore the possibility of using CPTED principles, concepts, and strategies (e.g., water barriers, landscaping) to install barriers. Consult Appendix A of Site and Urban Design for Security: Guidance against Potential Terrorist Attacks (FEMA 430) , available at www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf , and Tables 2.4 and 2.5 of Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06) , available at http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips- 06.pdf for more information. Source 1,

Employ temporary passive barriers (e.g., Jersey barriers) to eliminate straight-line vehicular access to the facility as needed (e.g., under special circumstances or during elevated threat situations).
Use vehicles as temporary physical barriers to eliminate straight-line vehicular access to the facility during elevated threat conditions.

Install K-rated barriers in areas vulnerable to invasive vehicles. Consult Site and Urban Design for Security: Guidance against Potential Terrorist Attacks (FEMA 430) , available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf , for more information. Source 1,

Explore the feasibility of upgrading all barriers to meet K-rated standards. Consult Site and Urban Design for Security: Guidance against Potential Terrorist Attacks (FEMA 430) , available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf , for more information. Source 1,

Install barriers to increase standoff distance and reduce damage from a potential explosive device. Options include, but are not limited to, fixed and retractable bollards, engineered planters, heavy objects and trees, walls and ha-ha barriers, water obstacles, and Jersey barriers.
Install fixed bollards, engineered planters, and/or heavy objects, such as boulders and sculptures, to increase standoff distance from a potential explosive device and reduce damage.

Explore alternatives if it is not possible to create sufficient standoff distance through the use of barriers. Options may include hardening the building and minimizing hazardous flying debris during an explosive event (e.g., window protective measures such as anti-shatter film, bullet-resistant glass, and laminated glass).
Employ temporary passive barriers (e.g., Jersey barriers) to increase standoff distance as needed (e.g., under special circumstances or during elevated threat conditions).
Use vehicles as temporary physical barriers to increase standoff distance during elevated threat conditions.

Explore the possibility of using CPTED principles, concepts, and strategies (e.g., water barriers, landscaping) to install barriers. Consult Appendix A of Site and Urban Design for Security: Guidance against Potential Terrorist Attacks (FEMA 430) , available at www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf , and Tables 2.4 and 2.5 of Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06) , available at http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips- 06.pdf for more information. Source 1 ,

Explore the possibility of using CPTED principles, concepts, and strategies (e.g., water barriers, landscaping) to install barriers. Consult Appendix A of Site and Urban Design for Security: Guidance against Potential Terrorist Attacks (FEMA 430) , available at www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf , and Tables 2.4 and 2.5 of Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06) , available at http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips- 06.pdf for more information.
