Conduct an assessment to determine additional appropriate, feasible mitigation measures to reduce the vulnerability and risk associated with flying glass during an explosive event. Options to consider include, but are not limited to, anti-shatter film, blast curtains, bullet-resistant glass, and laminated glass.

Apply anti-shatter film to existing glass to provide fragment retention and reduce the overall velocity of glass fragments at failure, especially near regularly occupied and mass-gathering areas. Anti-shatter film may mitigate the impact of wind-blown debris and seismic stress in addition to explosive force by preventing pieces of broken glass from becoming lethal projectiles.
Consult Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06) , available at http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf , for more information.
Install protective coverings, metal grills, and/or steel mesh to harden windows against penetration.

Determine whether windows are required. If not, explore the feasibility of permanently changing to a solid wall with no glazing, which would enhance penetration delay and help protect personnel, property, and operations from blast effects.
Replace glass doors with solid doors without glass panels, if possible.

Apply anti-shatter film to existing glass to provide fragment retention and reduce the overall velocity of glass fragments at failure, especially near regularly occupied and mass-gathering areas. Anti-shatter film may mitigate the impact of wind-blown debris and seismic stress in addition to explosive force by preventing pieces of broken glass from becoming lethal projectiles.
Relocate hinges on the interior, if possible, or provide concealed hinges to reduce the likelihood of tampering. Hinge pins could also be welded to hinge to preclude easy removal.
Secure hinges, hinge pins, and hasps against unauthorized removal by using spot welds or peened mounting bolts.
Install reinforced strike plates on facility doors to deter unauthorized forced entry.
Install screens or grates to ceiling/roof openings to deter entry.
Install intrusion detection sensors to detect attempts to penetrate or enter the facility through these openings.

Install protective measures, such as screens, to protect against malicious acts. Screens will prevent objects from being tossed into the intakes from the ground; such screens should be sloped to allow thrown objects to roll or slide off the screen, away from intakes.

Elevate accessible wall-mounted or below-ground intakes by constructing a plenum chamber or external shafts over the intakes. An extension height of 12 feet will place intakes out of reach of individuals without some assistance.
Install physical security measures, such as fencing, CCTV cameras, and motion detectors around air intakes to facilitate monitoring by security personnel.
Deploy the security force to regularly patrol and inspect outside air intakes for signs of malevolent activity, especially during elevated threat levels.

(Ref: OFC #3) Consult Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06) , available at http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf , for more information.
Consult Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06) , available at http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf , for more information.

Conduct an assessment to determine the most appropriate CBR detection system for the facility to protect against CBR threats. Consult Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06) , available at http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf , for more information.
Monitor CBR detection technologies, and carefully consider the costs and benefits of implementing such systems. If CBR detection technology is appropriate and approved for the facility:
Install CBR detectors in air ductwork systems and remote sensing systems in areas with large occupancy populations.
Place CBR detection technology on the building's exterior and interior.
Integrate CBR detection equipment into central security management control and building management systems so facility personnel are immediately notified of intentionally released hazards.
Inform local law enforcement and first responders of the facility's CBR countermeasures and associated emergency protocols.
Conduct an assessment to determine the most appropriate filtration system for the facility to protect against CBR threats.

Invest in advanced filtration systems that can afford a measure of protection against CBR threats. Incorporate solid-particle filters with high minimum efficiency reporting value (MERV) ratings for maximum efficiency and effective filtration.
Ensure that access ports are locked and secured.
Add monitoring and detection capabilities (e.g., CCTV cameras, IDS) to detect attempts to penetrate or enter the facility through these access ports.
Conduct frequent security checks of these access ports.
Permanently remove or block access to and from these access ports, if possible.
Ensure that access point(s) are continuously controlled by the security force or locked when not guarded.
Add intrusion detection and assessment capabilities (e.g., CCTV cameras, IDS) to detect unauthorized attempts to enter the facility through the access point(s).
Conduct frequent security checks of the access point(s) to the facility from such areas.
Permanently remove or block access to and from underground areas, if possible.

Evaluate the weakest areas of the exterior and install IDS sensors as appropriate. Types of sensors used to detect exterior intrusion include buried-line sensors, fence-associated sensors, and freestanding sensors.

Evaluate the weakest areas of the exterior and install IDS sensors as appropriate. The basic types of sensors used for exterior intrusion are buried-line sensors, fence-associated sensors, and freestanding sensors. The IDS should be evenly distributed throughout critical areas to ensure uniform detection around the entire length of the facility’s perimeter. Sensors should be placed in locations that avoid electromagnetic, acoustic, thermal, nuclear radiation, optical, seismic, and meteorological interferences.
Explore the feasibility of installing fence-associated sensors for the exterior IDS. Options may include, but are not limited to, electric fields, sensors, and disturbance wires.
Explore the feasibility of providing backup power to the IDS to ensure its continued operation during a loss of power to the facility.
Plan to increase security measures when the IDS is not operational, for example, increased roving patrols by the security force and/or additional access control measures.
Explore the feasibility of incorporating tampering and system issue indicators to the IDS to ensure facility personnel are notified of incidents that could render the IDS nonoperational.

Establish a clear detection zone. Perimeter IDS systems perform more effectively when located in a defined clear zone. Such areas should be cleared of aboveground obstacles and vegetation that might block the IDS receivers and transmitters.
Explore the feasibility of installing alarm enunciators for the IDS.
Contact the IDS manufacturer and/or installer to evaluate changes or upgrades that can be made to the system to allow for the incorporation of visual or audible alarm indicators.
Maintain the IDS according to the manufacturer's recommend specifications. Develop a maintenance log to document all repairs and part replacements for the IDS. Source 1 ,
Develop policies and procedures to periodically test the IDS and address inadequate test results. Maintain records of test results, issues, and the resolution of identified issues.

Evaluate the weakest points of entry into the facility and install IDS sensors as appropriate. Types of sensors used to detect interior intrusion include boundary penetration sensors, interior motion sensors, proximity sensors, door sensors, and window sensors.

Explore the feasibility of installing door sensors for critical areas within the facility. Options may include, but are not limited to, glass breakage sensors, grid mesh, vibration sensors, magnetic contacts, and conducting tape. Source 1, Source 2,

Explore the feasibility of installing window sensors, at least for critical areas within the facility. Options may include, but are not limited to, glass breakage sensors, grid mesh, vibration sensors, balanced magnetic contacts, and conducting tape. Source 1,
Explore the feasibility of providing backup power to the IDS to ensure its continued operation during a loss of power to the facility.

Implement policies and procedures for facility or security force personnel to assess alarms to evaluate the cause of activation. Timely alarm assessment is essential to effectively responding to intrusions. Source 1, Source 2,
Contact the IDS manufacturer and/or installer to evaluate changes or upgrades that can be made to the system to allow for the incorporation of visual or audible alarm indicators.
Maintain the IDS according to the manufacturer's recommend specifications. Develop a maintenance log to document all repairs and part replacements for the IDS. Source 1,
Develop policies and procedures to periodically test the IDS and address inadequate test results. Maintain records of test results, issues, and the resolution of identified issues.
Explore the feasibility of installing a comprehensive CCTV system onsite.

Explore the feasibility of installing a comprehensive CCTV system onsite. Site-specific factors must be considered when selecting components that comprise a particular CCTV system. For example, the size of the system, in terms of the number of cameras needed, is the minimum number required to view all electronic security system sensor detection fields. In addition, some cameras may require artificial light sources. Finally, performance criteria and physical, environmental, and economic considerations must be factored into the component selection.
Install CCTV cameras where they will capture an unobstructed field of view of the desired scene and where there is minimal risk of vandalism, sabotage, or environmental damage, if possible.

Evaluate the need to install CCTV cameras in the following locations outdoors: along site perimeter detection zone, at controlled access points, within the protected area, and to view approaches to critical assets.
Evaluate the need to install CCTV cameras in the following locations indoors: at selected assets within the protected area, and at critical portals adjacent to assets.
Provide for effective monitoring of the CCTV system based on the security requirements of the facility.

Evaluate the need for dedicated, trained security staff to monitor the CCTV system. Explore options to maximize the effectiveness of CCTV monitoring and observation, such as frequently rotating shifts for monitoring staff and limiting the number of cameras monitored by each staff member.

Determine the objectives of video recording and retention (e.g., verify information, prove it, or aid prosecution with it) to select the appropriate video recording and storage systems for the facility.
Evaluate CCTV coverage of the facility perimeter to determine if it meets the facility's security requirements. Explore options to increase coverage as necessary.
Evaluate CCTV coverage of areas of concern to determine if it meets the facility's security requirements. Explore options to increase coverage as necessary.
Evaluate CCTV coverage of critical areas/SAAs to determine if it meets the facility's security requirements. Explore options to increase coverage as necessary.
Explore the feasibility of providing backup power to the CCTV system to ensure its continued operation during a loss of power to the facility.
Explore the feasibility of upgrading the CCTV system to include video analytics or anomaly detection software.
Provide for more effective monitoring of the CCTV system based on the security requirements of the facility.

Evaluate the need for dedicated, trained security staff to monitor the CCTV system. Explore options to maximize the effectiveness of CCTV monitoring and observation, such as frequently rotating shifts for monitoring staff and limiting the number of cameras monitored by each staff member.
Evaluate the need for real-time monitoring of the CCTV system based on the security requirements of the facility.

Evaluate the need for dedicated, trained security staff to monitor the CCTV system. Explore options to maximize the effectiveness of CCTV monitoring and observation, such as frequently rotating shifts for monitoring staff and limiting the number of cameras monitored by each staff member.
Evaluate the need to record CCTV video, and select video recording and storage systems for the facility as appropriate.
Upgrade the CCTV system to provide for video recording and storage. Develop a policy for the review of recorded information (e.g., periodically or only after an incident).
Review the information recorded by the CCTV system periodically, to establish awareness of all incidents captured on camera.
Routinely review information recorded by the CCTV system after-hours (on fast-forward).
Explore the feasibility of upgrading the CCTV system to include video analytics or anomaly detection software. Source 1,
Evaluate the need to extend the time that recorded information from the CCTV system remains in storage. Explore options to increase the system's storage capacity as necessary. Source 1,
Evaluate the need to store recorded information from the CCTV system, and explore options to implement this capability as necessary.
Upgrade the CCTV system to provide for video storage. Develop a policy for the review of recorded information (e.g., periodically or only after an incident). Source 1,

Install lighting for fences, gates, and/or parking areas. Before installation, determine the appropriate type of lighting based on the overall requirements of the site and the building (for example, continuous or standby). In addition, consider operational costs, such as life-cycle costs for energy and maintenance, when designing an appropriate lighting situation, because of their effect on project sustainability. Source 1,
Explore the feasibility of implementing the following:
Installing two or more lighting units at pedestrian entrances, that provide adequate illumination for recognizing individuals and examining credentials.
Installing two lighting units at vehicle access points, to facilitate the complete inspection of passenger cars, trucks, etc., as well as their contents and passengers.

Providing a minimum surface lighting average of 4 horizontal foot-candles at gates to help ensure adequate lighting for pedestrians, islands, and guards in approach and exit zones. However, this level is inadequate in entry control zones where IDs will be checked. Guardhouse and ID check positions should have a minimum of 10 foot-candles, with 20 foot-candles preferred. (A foot-candle is a unit of illuminance on a surface that is everywhere 1 foot from a uniform point source of light of one candle and equal to 1 lumen per square foot. To convert foot-candles to watts, first convert foot-candles into lumens, then convert lumens into watts. One foot-candle equals 10.76 lumens, and 1 lumen equals 0.001496 watts.) Source 1,

Using glare projection for isolated fenced perimeters and keeping security patrol routes unlit. (Isolated fenced perimeters are fence lines around areas where the fence is 100 feet or more from buildings or operating areas. The approach area is clear of obstruction for 100 or more feet outside of the fence. Other personnel do not use the area.)

Using controlled lighting and keeping security patrol routes in relative darkness for semi-isolated fenced perimeters. (Semi-isolated, fenced perimeters are fence lines where approach areas are clear of obstruction for 60 to 100 feet outside of the fence. The general public or facility personnel seldom have reason to be in the area.)

Using controlled lighting for non-isolated fenced perimeters. It may not be practical to keep the patrol area dark. (Non-isolated fenced perimeters are fence lines immediately adjacent to operating areas. Outsiders or facility personnel may move about freely in this approach area.)
Ensure that lighting is compatible with CCTV systems, as the cameras may need high intensity, low intensity, or infrared light for proper operation.

Consult Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06) , available at http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf , for more information about security lighting.
Update the lighting system to include overlapping cones of light to eliminate or reduce shadows and dark areas.
Update the lighting system to ensure illumination uniformity.

Update the lighting system to ensure illumination uniformity, so that security personnel can see ahead and to the sides with an absence of dark areas caused by shadows. Lighting should be brightest in secure areas, with the light gradually less in areas adjacent to high-illumination areas.
Establish a maintenance protocol to ensure that system components are regularly inspected and repaired or replaced as necessary. Ensure that lighting fixtures are clean and properly aimed. Source 1,

Establish a maintenance protocol to ensure that system components are regularly inspected and repaired or replaced as necessary. Ensure that lighting fixtures are clean and properly aimed. Replace working bulbs on a schedule based on their life expectancy. Source 1,
Expand the coverage of the backup power supply to include emergency lighting for these areas, so that in the event of a power failure or other emergency, illumination is available.
Ensure that the backup power supply covers most existing lights, not only lights for critical locations.
Ensure that the backup power supply covers emergency lighting for most, if not all, critical locations.
Explore the feasibility of procuring portable lighting capabilities to provide coverage for these areas, as needed.

Install lighting for building entrances and delivery areas. Before installation, determine the appropriate type of lighting based on the overall requirements of the site and the building (for example, continuous or standby). In addition, consider operational costs, such as life-cycle costs for energy and maintenance because of their effect on project sustainability. Source 1,

Install lighting for waterside facilities. Before installation, determine the appropriate type of lighting based on the overall requirements of the site and the building (for example, continuous or standby). In addition, consider operational costs, such as life-cycle costs for energy and maintenance, when designing an appropriate lighting situation, because of their effect on project sustainability. Source 1,
Illuminating water approaches and the pier area. The lighting must not in any way violate marine rules and regulations (for example, it must not be glaring to pilots).

Illuminating decks on open piers to at least 1 foot-candle, and the water approaches (extending to a distance of 100 feet from the pier) to at least 0.5 foot-candles. (A foot-candle is a unit of illuminance on a surface that is everywhere 1 foot from a uniform point source of light of one candle and equal to 1 lumen per square foot. To convert foot-candles to watts, first convert foot-candles into lumens, then convert lumens into watts. One foot-candle equals 10.76 lumens, and 1 lumen equals 0.001496 watts.) Source 1,
Illuminating the area beneath the pier floor with small-wattage floodlights arranged on the piling.
Providing portable lighting as part of the protective lighting system for piers and docks.
Consult the U.S. Coast Guard for approval of protective lighting adjacent to navigable waters.

Reach out to the utility provider to determine the substation(s) that services the facility. Maintaining regular communication channels with utility service providers will enhance the facility's understanding of actions to be taken in response to a loss of service from primary supply sources, restoration priorities, and potential alternatives.

Collaborate with the electric power utility provider on the feasibility of installing a second service connection that enters the facility in a different location to create redundancy. It is preferred that a secondary connection be serviced from a different substation and that the connection be able to support the entire load of the facility.
Install an emergency generator capable of providing adequate power for the entire facility load during an extended commercial power outage.
Collaborate with electric power utility provider to explore options to modify service connections such that any one connection could meet the entire facility load.
Explore the feasibility of separating the electric service connections so they enter the facility at different locations.
Explore the feasibility of relocating the electric service connection(s) to help mitigate the risk that the catastrophic failure of one utility could inadvertently take down another.
Secure components of the electrical system inside the facility against unauthorized access, for example, with access-controlled utility closets and locked electrical boxes.
Evaluate the need to implement enhanced protective measures for electrical system components, such as intrusion detection sensors and CCTV.
Conduct random security checks of critical interior utility closets and assets.
Secure components of the electrical system outside of the facility against unauthorized access, for example, with door locks.

Install a fence or protective structure around exterior electrical system equipment (e.g., transformers, switchgear) to secure them against unauthorized access and to decrease the risk of accidental or intentional damage to these critical components.
Evaluate the need to implement enhanced protective measures for components of the electrical system, such as intrusion detection sensors and CCTV.
Conduct random security checks of critical exterior electrical system equipment.
Install vehicle barriers to protect electric power equipment from damage.
Explore the feasibility of prohibiting parking near electric power equipment. Utility systems should be located at least 50 feet from loading docks, front entrances, and parking areas.

Determine the electric power requirements to support facility core operations, and develop commensurate plans in collaboration with the utility provider to ensure service is restored with priority, or alternate service provisions are made. Ensure plans address legitimate hypothetical scenarios that would result in service loss and corresponding restoration timelines so the facility can address business continuity and interim workarounds.

Collaborate with the utility provider to determine if the utility prioritizes certain types of facilities for restoration before other customers in the event of a service disruption, and if the facility is eligible for priority restoration.

If the facility is not eligible for priority restoration before other customers, establish a contingency/business continuity plan to resume facility operations after a service disruption. As part of the planning process, collaborate with the utility provider to review options for rapid restoration of service. Establish a liaison with the utility provider to maintain communications during a service outage. Ensure the contingency/business continuity plan is well understood by facility management and employees.
Evaluate power requirements for the facility to maintain core operations, and install backup power equipment accordingly. At a minimum, ensure all life safety services have backup power.

Conduct an assessment to determine which systems and equipment are essential to the continued operation of the facility. They may include one or more of the following: HVAC systems; industrial equipment and major appliances; lights (interior and exterior), computers, and other office equipment; pumps, including sump pumps, sprinkler system pumps, and well water pumps; and alarm systems. Once essential systems and equipment have been identified, determine how much power they require, and then consult with a generator technical expert regarding the appropriate size and type of generator(s) for the facility. The technical expert can also help select other components of the emergency power system, including the main transfer switch and the electrical panel.

Explore options to enter into a contract to have a backup power generator readily available, if installing a generator onsite is not feasible. Ensure that the facility is equipped (i.e., has the necessary wiring and transfer switches) to facilitate the rapid installation and operation of the generator unit.

If the installation of three or more backup power generators is planned, consider installing them in two separate locations to mitigate the vulnerability of a single point of failure. Similarly, the electric utility switchgear, the automatic transfer switch, and the backup switchgear should be in separate locations with sufficient separation distance.
Consult the FEMA flyer Install a Generator for Emergency Power , available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-0643/how2022_generator_04_11_r1.pdf , for more information.

Develop contracts/procedures for refueling the backup power generator during emergencies. Consider the possibility that the primary supplier may not be available, and establish contracts with alternate fuel providers, if possible.
The backup power generator provides power only for life safety services such as emergency lighting: therefore, a loss of commercial electric power would significantly disrupt facility operations.
Upgrade the backup power system so security systems such as IDS and CCTV may also receive emergency power in the event of a commercial power outage.
Explore the feasibility of increasing the capacity of the backup power system to support at least core operations and life safety services when the primary power supply is disrupted.

Develop routine load test policies and procedures for emergency power generators. Use manufacturer’s recommendations and industry standards to determine frequency, duration, and percent of load for tests.
Develop a plan to maintain and test the backup power generator regularly.
Review the manufacturer’s recommendations and industry standards to determine if the backup power generator requires more frequent testing.
Collaborate with natural gas utility provider on the feasibility of installing a second service connection that enters the facility in a different location to create redundancy.
Collaborate with the natural gas utility provider on the feasibility of modifying service connections such that any one connection could meet the entire facility load.
Explore the feasibility of separating the natural gas service connections so that they enter the facility at different locations.
Explore the feasibility of relocating the natural gas service connection(s) to help mitigate the risk that the catastrophic failure of one utility could inadvertently take down another.
Secure components of the natural gas system inside of the facility against unauthorized access, for example, with access-controlled utility closets.
Evaluate the need to implement enhanced protective measures for natural gas system components, such as intrusion detection sensors and CCTV.
Conduct random security checks of critical interior utility closets and assets.
Secure components of the natural gas system outside of the facility against unauthorized access.
Conduct random security checks of critical exterior natural gas system equipment.
Install vehicle barriers to protect the natural gas main and associated equipment from damage.

Explore the feasibility of prohibiting parking near the natural gas main and associated equipment. Utility systems should be located at least 50 feet from loading docks, front entrances, and parking areas.

Determine the natural gas supply requirements to support facility core operations, and develop commensurate plans in collaboration with the utility provider to ensure service is restored with priority, or alternate service provisions are made. Ensure plans address legitimate hypothetical scenarios that would result in service loss and corresponding restoration timelines so the facility can address business continuity and interim workarounds.

Collaborate with the utility provider to determine if the utility prioritizes certain types of facilities for restoration before other customers in the event of a service disruption, and if the facility is eligible for priority restoration.

If the facility is not eligible for priority restoration before other customers, establish a contingency/business continuity plan to resume facility operations after a service disruption. As part of the planning process, collaborate with the utility provider to review options for rapid restoration of service. Establish a liaison with the utility provider to maintain communications during a service outage. Ensure the contingency/business continuity plan is well understood by facility management and employees.

Explore the feasibility of establishing an internal natural gas source or an alternative fuel source (e.g., diesel fuel, propane, electricity) to support at least core operations when the external natural gas supply is disrupted.
Collaborate with water utility service provider on the feasibility of installing a second service connection that enters the facility in a different location to create redundancy.
Consult with the water utility provider and inquire about the feasibility of installing an additional connection that is capable of handling the entire facility load.
Explore the feasibility of relocating the water service connection(s) to help mitigate the risk that the catastrophic failure of one utility could inadvertently take down another.
Secure components of the water system inside the facility against unauthorized access, for example, with access-controlled utility closets.
Evaluate the need to implement enhanced protective measures for water system components, such as intrusion detection sensors and CCTV.
Secure components of the water system outside of the facility against unauthorized access.

Determine if access to water supply system components is required for maintenance. If so, secure openings with screening, grating, latticework, or other similar devices so that access points do not allow unauthorized access.

Secure manhole covers 10 inches or more in diameter, for example, with locks and hasps, by welding them shut, or by appropriate bolting to their frames. Ensure that hasps, locks, and bolts are made of materials that resist corrosion. Keyed bolts (which make removal by unauthorized personnel more difficult) are also available.
(Ref: OFC #6) Evaluate the need to implement enhanced protective measures for water system components, such as intrusion detection sensors and CCTV.
Conduct random security checks of critical exterior water system assets.
Install vehicle barriers to protect the water supply from damage.
Explore the feasibility of prohibiting parking near the water supply. Utility systems should be located at least 50 feet from loading docks, front entrances, and parking areas.

Determine the water supply requirements to support facility core operations, and develop commensurate plans in collaboration with the utility provider to ensure service is restored with priority, or alternate service provisions are made. Ensure plans address legitimate hypothetical scenarios that would result in service loss and corresponding restoration timelines so the facility can address business continuity and interim workarounds.

Cyber incident reports can be made to request technical assistance, request incident management support, and/or for information sharing. Information sharing plays a key role in the prevention of cyber attacks and ensures new threats can be identified quickly and effective countermeasures can be developed to neutralize/defeat them. Cyber threats/attack techniques are continuously evolving and change on a daily basis; many organizations/companies may be unaware of these latest threats. Participating in information sharing with other outside organizations and facilities may reduce this vulnerability and could possibly prevent a future cybersecurity attack from being successful.

Identification and classification of cyber critical assets: Identify the mission- or business-critical functions and identify the resources that support the critical functions. Source 1,

Access control policies: access control is necessary to ensure that use of the system or information is provided only to authorized users who have identified and authenticated themselves to obtain access. Consult NIST Interagency Report 7316: Assessment of Access Control Systems , available here , for more information. Source 1 ,

IT security roles and responsibilities: Defining clear roles and responsibilities is necessary to ensure that cyber security is properly managed and executed, that all necessary duties are assigned, that personnel receive the proper training, and that personnel have only the privileges necessary to fulfill their assignments. Source 1 ,

IT security training: Personnel need sufficient training, based upon their roles and responsibilities, to ensure they can perform their jobs without introducing security vulnerabilities. Consult NIST SP 800-53 Revision 4: Security and Privacy Controls for Federal Information Systems and Organizations , available here , for more information.

Audit trails: Without an audit trail, an organization might fail to detect security compromises, misuse of information, or other security-related events. Consult NIST SP 800-53 Revision 4: Security and Privacy Controls for Federal Information Systems and Organizations , available here , for more information.

Disposal of protected assets: Sensitive information must be removed using proper sanitation methods, or media destruction, to ensure information is not released during disposal of protected assets. Consult NIST SP 800-53 Revision 4: Security and Privacy Controls for Federal Information Systems and Organizations , available here , for more information.

Incident response/management: This element captures the means of the facility to detect and respond to five categories of cyber incidents: Unauthorized Access, DOS, Malicious Code, Improper Usage, and Scans/Probes/Attempted Access. The incidents listed can be detected by multiple technical means including intrusion detection/prevention systems, firewalls, anti-virus tools, and vulnerability detection/assessment tools. Managing responses to incidents is necessary to ensure that incidents are properly detected and reported so that the incident is handled properly, forensic evidence is not lost, and essential operations are recovered as soon as possible.

Unauthorized access, i.e., an individual gains logical or physical access without permission to a network, system, application, data, or other resource. Unauthorized access may result in release of sensitive information or disruption of business operations.

DOS, i.e., an attack that successfully prevents or impairs the normal authorized functionality of networks, systems, or applications by exhausting resources. This activity includes being the victim or participating in the DOS. Defending against DOS attacks can benefit from detecting the presence of an attack and receiving external support to block incoming network traffic.
Malicious code, i.e., the successful installation of malicious software (e.g., virus, worm, Trojan horse, or other code-based malicious entity) that infects an operating system or application.

Improper usage, i.e., a person violates acceptable computing use policies. Improper usage can result in the leak of sensitive information, the introduction of malicious code, the loss of system or information integrity, or other security-related events.

Scan/probes/attempted access, i.e., any activity that seeks to access or identify a computer, open parts, protocols, service, or any combination for later exploit. Use intrusion detection and prevention software or network behavior analysis software to detect anomalous external network traffic, such as scanning. Participate in regional information sharing to receive information about active scanning or network-based security attacks.

Scan/probes/attempted access, i.e., any activity that seeks to access or identify a computer, open parts, protocols, service, or any combination for later exploit. Use intrusion detection and prevention software or network behavior analysis software to detect anomalous external network traffic, such as scanning. Security testing: Testing is necessary to verify and validate that the intended security rules are properly implemented. Vulnerabilities include software holes that a hacker might take advantage of, out of date virus definition files, default passwords set by a vendor, or accounts that are not password protected. Software designed for scanning, such as ISS or Nessus, is commonly used, as is active scan systems, which detect vulnerabilities as soon as a system joins the network.

Physical security of IT security assets: Without proper physical and environmental security, information systems are vulnerable to intentional attacks or accidents that can disruption their operations and release sensitive information. Source 1,

Firewalls: Firewalls are devices or programs that control the flow of network traffic between networks or hosts that employ differing security postures. Without a firewall, internal systems are exposed to potential attacks against which they might not have sufficient protection. Consult NIST SP 800-41: Guidelines on Firewalls and Firewall Policy , available here , for more information.
Electronic communications

Remote access: Remote access introduces numerous vulnerabilities ranging from lack of physical security on remote access devices (home computers, phones, tables), potential malware on those remote devices, information travels over unsecured networks (Internet), and opening internal resources to external devices. Securing remote access requires encrypting traffic from the remote devices, using technologies such as VPN, and strictly enforcing authentication. Consult NIST SP 800-46: Guide to Enterprise Telework and Remote Access Security , available here , for more information.

Wireless connectivity: Wireless technologies are typically less secure than wired technologies, primarily because they can extend outside of the physical footprint of the organization, but also because they often have weak security configurations. A best-case scenario would be for wireless to not be used at all, especially on control networks. Other scenarios may have a separate visitor network space, such that a visitor would not be able to scan traffic on the internal network. Employees would need to VPN or authenticate in some manner to gain access. A worst-case scenario would be that wireless is open to all, and exists on the same network as the internal systems. Consult NIST SP 800-153: Guidelines for Securing Wireless Local Area Networks (WLANs) , available here , for more information on securing wireless technology.

Security patches or updates: A "patch" is a piece of computer code that a software company writes and distributes to fix a problem found in one of its previously released programs. Many patches fix problems related to security—specifically, vulnerabilities in the programs that attackers can exploit. Hackers seek out these vulnerabilities to gain access to a computer and its information, which then can be used to gain access to other vulnerable computers and information. These compromised computers also can be used to attack other computers. To prevent these problems, patches need to be deployed to computer systems quickly to minimize the window of opportunity for attackers. Consult NIST SP 800-40 Revision 3: Guide to Enterprise Patch Management Technologies , available here , for more information.

Conduct regular cybersecurity exercises. An exercise is a simulation of an emergency designed to validate the viability of one or more aspects of an IT plan. In an exercise, personnel with roles and responsibilities in a particular IT plan meet to validate the content of a plan through discussion of their roles and their responses to emergency situations, execution of responses in a simulated operational environment, or other means of validating responses that does not involve using the actual operational environment. Exercises are scenario-driven, such as a power failure in one of the organization’s data centers or a fire causing certain systems to be damaged, with additional situations often being presented during the course of an exercise.

Document the results from cybersecurity exercises. Critically evaluate exercises to assess whether goals and objectives were achieved, the effectiveness of participation, and the adequacy of cybersecurity response policies and procedures in the event of a real cybersecurity attack. Modify cybersecurity plans, policies, and procedures as necessary.

Implement LAN segmentation physically or with VLANs. Use routing to provide systems cross-connectivity only where necessary. Employ access control measures to restrict access between segmented networks. Managed interfaces include, for example, gateways, routers, firewalls, guards, network-based malicious code analysis and virtualization systems, and encrypted tunnels implemented within a security architecture (e.g., routers protecting firewalls or application gateways residing on protected subnetworks). Restricting or prohibiting interfaces within organizational information systems includes, for example, restricting external Web traffic to designated Web servers within managed interfaces and prohibiting external traffic that appears to be spoofing internal addresses.
